title: Forking & Contribution Workflow
description: The forking workflow is the standard contribution model for open source on GitHub. A contributor forks the upstream repository, clones their fork, creates feature branches, commits changes, and submits a pull request back to the original project. This guide covers the full lifecycle from both contributor and maintainer perspectives.

sections.title: Forking vs Branching
sections.description:
Forking creates a copy of the entire repository under your GitHub account, allowing independent development without affecting the original. Branching creates a parallel line of development within the same repository. Forks are the standard for external contributions to repositories where you lack write access.

When to fork: contributing to external projects, contributing without write access, experimenting with major changes, maintaining a custom version. When to branch: team members with write access, internal feature development, paired with branch protection rules.

sections.title: Sync with Upstream
sections.description:
After forking and cloning, configure the upstream remote — git remote add upstream https://github.com/original/owner/repo.git. Sync your fork's main branch regularly to avoid drift: git fetch upstream then git rebase upstream/main on your feature branch.

Keeping your fork in sync prevents merge conflicts when submitting PRs. Use git pull --rebase upstream main as a shortcut. Many projects recommend rebasing over merging to maintain a linear, clean history.

sections.title: Contribution Best Practices
sections.description:
Before starting work: check open issues and existing PRs to avoid duplication, comment on the issue to signal intent, and review CONTRIBUTING.md. Create small, focused PRs — each PR should address a single concern. Write clear commit messages following Conventional Commits.

Checklist: follow code style (linter), add tests for new functionality, update documentation, ensure CI passes, include before/after screenshots if UI changes, and reference the issue number in the PR description.

sections.title: Review & Iteration
sections.description:
After submitting a PR, maintainers will review the code. Be responsive to feedback: address review comments, push new commits to the same branch (they'll appear in the PR automatically), and use git rebase -i to clean up history if requested. Maintainers may use squash and merge to collapse your commits.

If CI fails, investigate the failing checks and push fixes. For large PRs, maintainers may request smaller, focused PRs. If the PR goes stale, rebase on latest upstream and re-request review.

sections.title: Maintainer Perspective
sections.description:
From the maintainer side: review PRs for correctness, test coverage, documentation, and adherence to project conventions. Use GitHub's review features — inline comments, approval changes, and request changes. CI must pass before merge. Prefer squash merge for external contributions to maintain a clean history.

Tools for maintainers: gh pr review --approve, CODEOWNERS for automatic reviewer assignment, merge queues for linear history, and semantic PR titles for automated changelog generation. Welcome first-time contributors with encouraging feedback.

details:
The forking workflow is the foundation of open source collaboration on GitHub. It enables any developer to contribute to any public repository without coordination overhead, creating the decentralized contribution model that powers the open source ecosystem.

Step-by-Step Forking Workflow

  1. Fork — click the Fork button on the upstream repository on GitHub
  2. Clone — git clone https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/repo.git
  3. Add upstream — git remote add upstream https://github.com/original/repo.git
  4. Create branch — git checkout -b feature/my-change
  5. Commit — make changes and commit with descriptive messages
  6. Push — git push origin feature/my-change
  7. Open PR — navigate to your fork on GitHub and click "Compare & pull request"

Handling Merge Conflicts

When upstream changes conflict with your PR, rebase your branch: git fetch upstream && git rebase upstream/main. Resolve conflicts with git mergetool or manually, then git rebase --continue. Force push is required after rebase: git push --force-with-lease.

Maintainer Merge Strategies

GitHub offers three merge strategies: Create a merge commit (preserves full history), Squash and merge (collapses all PR commits into one — preferred for external contributions), and Rebase and merge (replays commits onto base, requires linear history). Use merge queues for repositories with many contributions to ensure CI passes before merge.

Code Examples

# Full forking workflow
# Step 1: Clone your fork
git clone https://github.com/kallol/repo.git
cd repo

# Step 2: Add upstream remote
git remote add upstream https://github.com/original/repo.git
git remote -v  # confirm both origin and upstream

# Step 3: Sync main branch
git checkout main
git pull --rebase upstream main
git push origin main

# Step 4: Create feature branch
git checkout -b fix/login-bug

# Step 5: Make changes, commit, push
git add .
git commit -m "fix: resolve OAuth timeout on Safari 17"
git push origin fix/login-bug

# Step 6: Open PR via gh CLI
gh pr create --title "fix: resolve OAuth timeout on Safari 17" \n  --body "Fixes #123" \n  --base main

# Step 7: Address review feedback
git add .
git commit -m "review: handle edge case for empty token"
git push origin fix/login-bug

# Step 8: Clean up after merge
git checkout main
git pull --rebase upstream main
git branch -d fix/login-bug
git push origin --delete fix/login-bug
cheatSheet:
Core Commands

  • git remote add upstream <url> — link your fork to the original repo.
  • git fetch upstream — sync upstream refs without merging.
  • git rebase upstream/main — replay your feature commits atop the latest upstream.
  • git push --force-with-lease — safe force-push after rebasing.
  • gh pr create — open a PR from the command line.

Staff+ Patterns

  • Keep forks in sync daily — drift is the #1 cause of merge conflicts in community PRs.
  • Use git pull --rebase upstream main as a single-command sync habit.
  • Maintainers: prefer squash-merge for external contributions to keep history clean.
  • For large features, open a draft PR early — signals intent and invites early feedback.

Watch Outs

  • Never force-push to a shared branch in the upstream repo — only to your fork.
  • git push --force (without --with-lease) overwrites remote refs blindly. Always use --force-with-lease.
  • Rebasing a PR that has comments or reviews resets the review state on GitHub.

Interview Angle

GitHub L5–L6 asks about fork-based contribution at scale: "Design the system that handles 1M forks." Key talking points: object deduplication via alternates, shared object storage, and the fork event webhook. Also be ready to discuss the trade-off between forking (external collaboration) vs branching (internal collaboration).
